improvement_actions.action: Introduce dedicated SX-subtype probing questions that distinguish Sexual/SX instinct from Social and Self-Preservation stacking. The current question set never directly probed intensity of one-on-one bonds, merging behavior, or the SX drive for depth and chemistry in close relationships. Add at least two questions targeting whether the respondent seeks intense dyadic connection versus group belonging versus personal security.
improvement_actions.evidence: Exchanges [9], [10], [21], and [25] contain clear SX-subtype signals (deep one-on-one loyalty, the Reuben parking-lot disclosure, 'I don't lay it out flat for anybody,' Denise seeing 'the edges') that were never followed up with explicit SX differentiation questions, contributing to the 3/10 subtype_accuracy score.
improvement_actions.commandment_alignment: Ask what the transcript cannot yet see
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Expected to raise subtype_accuracy from 3/10 toward 7-8/10 by surfacing the SX instinct's signature patterns rather than leaving them as ambient signal in generic loyalty questions.

improvement_actions.action: Remove or restructure all binary forced-choice questions that offer only two labeled options and instead allow the respondent to self-generate the contrast. Forced binaries (as used in exchanges [2], [3], [5], [7], [8], [10], [11], [13], [14], [15], [16], [17], [18], [22], [23], [24]) pre-load the frame and violate the commandment against leading the witness. Replace with open-ended prompts followed by a reflective paraphrase check.
improvement_actions.evidence: Fourteen of twenty-five exchanges use binary forced-choice framing (e.g., [2]: 'Trust the read and act on it / Keep looking for proof before I move'; [7]: 'Setting the record straight / Making sure I'm protected'). The 4/10 commandment_fidelity score directly reflects this structural over-reliance on leading binaries that hand the respondent pre-interpreted language.
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Raising commandment_fidelity from 4/10 toward 8/10 and improving convergent_validity by ensuring type signals emerge organically rather than from assessor-supplied labels.

improvement_actions.action: Develop a mid-assessment pivot protocol that detects when a respondent's language has already confirmed a core type and shifts questioning toward differentiation of adjacent types and stress/integration patterns. Once Type 6 was strongly indicated by exchanges [1]-[4], subsequent questions should have introduced Type 8 and Type 1 contrast probes to rule out misidentification rather than continuing to accumulate confirmatory 6 evidence.
improvement_actions.evidence: By exchange [6] the transcript had accumulated strong 6w7 evidence (hypervigilance, coalition-building, authority ambivalence, scanning for next threat). Exchanges [7]-[23] largely re-confirmed the same signals. No question ever probed 8-style territorial dominance, 1-style internal critic, or 6-to-9 security behavior, leaving the question_quality score at 5/10 for redundancy and missed differentiation.
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Improving question_quality from 5/10 toward 8/10 and strengthening convergent_validity by stress-testing the type hypothesis rather than only confirming it.

improvement_actions.action: Add explicit health-level probing questions targeting the distinction between Level 5 (defensive, reactive, divisive alliances) and adjacent levels. Specifically, probe whether the respondent experiences their vigilance as draining or generative, whether they feel genuinely supported by their alliances or secretly uncertain of them, and whether worst-case thinking feels protective or compulsive. These probes should appear in the final third of the assessment.
improvement_actions.evidence: Exchange [19] ('The quiet makes me suspicious... someone somewhere is getting ready to pull something'), exchange [24] ('Did I give someone more credit than they deserved'), and exchange [25] ('Whether the story I've been telling about myself holds up') all encode Level 5 health signals — defensive suspicion, projection risk, identity instability — but the system never explicitly surfaced or named the health dimension, leaving it as ambient inference rather than assessed data.
improvement_actions.commandment_alignment: See the whole person, not just the type
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Enabling reliable health-level scoring as a distinct output dimension, improving clinical utility and allowing the system to flag when a respondent may benefit from support resources.

improvement_actions.action: Insert one explicit meta-reflection question near the assessment midpoint that invites the respondent to name what they notice about how they are engaging with the assessment itself — hesitations, things left unsaid, or patterns in their own answers. This surfaces meta-cognitive access and provides a validity check on the respondent's self-report accuracy.
improvement_actions.evidence: Exchange [25] produced the richest self-aware material in the transcript ('whether the story I've been telling about myself holds up') only because the question was unusually intimate, not because the system systematically created space for meta-reflection. improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Raising meta_awareness from 6/10 toward 8/10 and improving overall transcript richness by creating a deliberate opening for the respondent to surface blind spots or reservations that binary questions structurally suppress.

*exhales slowly, shifts in seat*

Something clicks on, I guess. Like a switch. I start running through it — who said what, what changed, what I might've missed. It's not panic exactly, more like... alertness. Heightened alertness, that's probably the right phrase for it. 

I've been in enough situations at the table — negotiating contracts, dealing with management that smiles too much — where you learn pretty quick that if something feels off, it usually is. So I trust that. I take it seriously.

*pauses, then adds dryly*

Whether I do anything useful with it, that's a different question.

When you sense something is off with a person — not what they said, but something underneath it — do you trust that read, or do you keep looking for proof?

- Trust the read and act on it
- Keep looking for proof before I move

You call it out. That's the short answer.

But it's not like you just walk in guns blazing, y'know — there's a way to do it that actually works and a way that just makes you feel righteous for about forty-five minutes before everything blows up in your face. transcript.content:
**Read the room first — I need to know who I can trust before I commit.**

And look, that's not hesitation — don't misread it as that. Charging in blind is how you hand people ammunition. You spend sixty seconds, maybe ninety, and you figure out who's got an agenda, who's nervous, who's actually listening. *Then* you move. The times I've ignored that instinct and just grabbed the wheel... I mean, let's just say I learned some things the hard way. You can control a room a lot better once you know which floor it's built on.
The people who've really gotten through to you — not the ones you respect, but the ones who've actually *reached* you — what did they do that most people don't?

transcript.content:
*pauses, taps fingers once on the table*

They didn't try to impress me, for one thing. And they didn't... perform concern, you know what I mean? Most people, they're asking you something but they're already halfway to their next point. The ones who actually got through — they just waited. Let the silence sit there without rushing to fill it. 

My old shop steward, Reuben, back in '94 — guy barely said ten words at a stretch. But he'd look at you like whatever you were saying was the only thing happening in the world right now. I told him things in a parking lot after a late meeting that I hadn't told my own wife at the time. That's probably... I don't know if that says something good or bad about me, honestly.

*small, dry laugh*

I guess what I'm saying is — they didn't need anything back from me while I was talking. That's rarer than it sounds.

**Can't stand the injustice going unanswered — that's what drives me.**

Though I'll say — and this is maybe where it gets complicated — I've walked into fights where the cause was clear as day, and then watched it fall apart because the people around me weren't who I thought they were. - The unreliability changes the calculus — I need to trust the people to fully commit
